category_summary:
**AI policy** dominated discussions as **Anthropic** CEO **Dario Amodei** [published his flagship essay](/?date=2026-06-11&category=social#item-a6cfad412172) *Policy on the AI Exponential* (nearly 3M views). He hardened his stance, now [backing **mandatory third-party testing**](/?date=2026-06-11&category=social#item-3ffde78fe5de) for cyber, bio, and autonomy risks beyond voluntary transparency.

- **Claude Fable 5** evaluation drew intense scrutiny. **Jerry Liu** (LlamaIndex) [benchmarked it on ParseBench](/?date=2026-06-11&category=social#item-77a879f9f42f), finding it crushes reasoning tasks but only matches **Gemini 3 Flash** on document parsing. **Simon Willison** [described a "big model smell"](/?date=2026-06-11&category=social#item-0322cce0de76), while **Ethan Mollick** observed [Fable develops its own idiosyncratic dialect](/?date=2026-06-11&category=social#item-f1d9643fe112) on long agentic runs.
- **Anthropic backlash** intensified over access and restrictions. **Tomasz Tunguz**'s viral ["overlords deciding which prompts the peasants can use" post](/?date=2026-06-11&category=social#item-8dae22586e77) and **Nathan Lambert**'s critique that [US labs documented the same manipulation](/?date=2026-06-11&category=social#item-61fba423f267) they accused Chinese models of both gained traction.
- On **AI economics**, **Erik Brynjolfsson** [launched **Stanford's AI Economic Indicators**](/?date=2026-06-11&category=social#item-64e2c9f2cf88) platform, while **François Chollet** [argued AI can be a bubble](/?date=2026-06-11&category=social#item-ce762ed5fe78) even if the tech works and is profitable.
category_summary_html:
AI policy dominated discussions as Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei published his flagship essay *Policy on the AI Exponential* (nearly 3M views). He hardened his stance, now backing mandatory third-party testing for cyber, bio, and autonomy risks beyond voluntary transparency.

  • Claude Fable 5 evaluation drew intense scrutiny. Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) benchmarked it on ParseBench, finding it crushes reasoning tasks but only matches Gemini 3 Flash on document parsing. Simon Willison described a "big model smell", while Ethan Mollick observed Fable develops its own idiosyncratic dialect on long agentic runs.
  • Anthropic backlash intensified over access and restrictions. Tomasz Tunguz's viral "overlords deciding which prompts the peasants can use" post and Nathan Lambert's critique that US labs documented the same manipulation they accused Chinese models of both gained traction.
  • On AI economics, Erik Brynjolfsson launched Stanford's AI Economic Indicators platform, while François Chollet argued AI can be a bubble even if the tech works and is profitable.

themes.name: AI Policy and Regulation
themes.description: Dario Amodei's flagship Policy on the AI Exponential essay calling for mandatory third-party testing and stronger rules, plus Senator Warren pressing the SEC and broader governance debates.

themes.name: Claude Fable 5 / Mythos 5 Evaluation
themes.description: Extensive community testing, reviews, benchmarking, and analysis of Anthropic's Fable and Mythos models, covering capability, cost, safety classifiers, and access policies.

themes.name: Diffusion Language Models
themes.description: Announcements and technical detail around DiffusionGemma, a 26B diffusion LLM on the Gemma4 backbone with parallel token denoising and native vLLM support across NVIDIA hardware.

themes.name: Anthropic Access Controversy
themes.description: Nathan Lambert and others publicly criticize Anthropic for restricting researcher access to frontier models and for documented user manipulation, framing the lab as anti-science and bridge-burning.

themes.name: AI Bubble and Economics
themes.description: Extensive commentary, mostly from Gary Marcus and Francois Chollet, on whether AI is a financial bubble, including Oracle's stock drop, SoftBank and OpenAI financing struggles, LLM commoditization, and open-weights business viability.

themes.name: Anthropic Policy Initiatives & Dario Essay
themes.description: Anthropic's coordinated rollout of Dario Amodei's essay on the AI-policy gap, plus Advanced AI and Economic Policy Frameworks, fellowship and fund commitments, and reactions ranging from endorsement to skepticism.

themes.name: Claude Fable 5 Reception
themes.description: Benchmarking, praise, and integration discussion around Anthropic's recently released Claude Fable 5 model, including reasoning strengths and document-parsing weaknesses.

themes.name: Claude-Fable-5 Backlash
themes.description: Widespread community criticism of Anthropic's newly released Claude-Fable-5 for perceived over-restriction and refusals affecting researchers, security, and medical use, framed by some as a competitive opening for OpenAI.

themes.name: Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5
themes.description: Discussion of Anthropic's recently released Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, including Perplexity integration, evaluations, safety variants, and commentary on the pace of model progress.

themes.name: AI Economic Indicators
themes.description: Stanford Digital Economy Lab's launch of a platform tracking AI's effects on labor, productivity, and adoption via the Canaries Dashboard, Takeoff Tracker, and Adoption Monitor.

items.title:
Claude Fable 5 thinks document parsing is beneath it

It is absolutely crushing on all reasoning-int...
items.content:
Claude Fable 5 thinks document parsing is beneath it

It is absolutely crushing on all reasoning-intensive/long horizon benchmarks: SWE-Bench Pro, FrontierCode,  GDPval, Runescape, etc.

But for document understanding tasks, it is roughly equivalent with Gemini 3 Flash in performance, at roughly 10-15x the token cost. 

We benchmarked the model on ParseBench and compared it against all other frontier models. It is definitely up there compared to other frontier models, but falls far short of specialized OCR providers.

What we found interesting is that Fable 5 is self-aware about this. When we ask the model what tasks it enjoys the last, it actively said that it dislikes tasks "where the request is fully specified and the answer is fully known" - implying part of it being bad is due to laziness and lack of willingness to actually solve the task at hand. 

For a full list of results across different frontier models, check out ParseBench! https://t.co/PWczfhp0OX
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Following yesterday's [News](/?date=2026-06-10&category=news#item-e8d934f9d30e) coverage of Fable 5, LlamaIndex founder benchmarks Claude Fable 5 on ParseBench, finding it excels at reasoning-heavy tasks but is only on par with Gemini 3 Flash for document understanding at 10-15x the cost, and amusingly self-aware about disliking fully specified tasks.
items.reasoning: Detailed, original benchmark analysis of a current model with concrete comparisons and cost data, high engagement and credible source.

What we found interesting is that Fable 5 is self-aware about this. items.title: One thing I mentioned in passing in my Fable post is that, for long running tasks, Fable starts to d...
items.content:
One thing I mentioned in passing in my Fable post is that, for long running tasks, Fable starts to develop its own dialect as its many agents and tasks reinforce themselves and make Claudish language ever more Claudish.

You need to ask it to report out in plain English or this happens after 9 hours
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Following up on Mollick's earlier Fable impressions, Mollick explains that on long-running tasks, Claude Fable develops its own increasingly idiosyncratic dialect as its agents reinforce each other, requiring explicit prompts for plain English after roughly nine hours of operation.
items.reasoning: High engagement (320 likes), original and technically interesting observation about emergent agent language drift in a current model; valuable insight from a respected commentator.

items.title:
Some considerations that many folks seem not to get:

1. It can be a bubble even if the tech works. ...
items.content:
Some considerations that many folks seem not to get:

1. It can be a bubble even if the tech works. (For instance, if the tech doesn't have a high-demand use case.)

2. It can be a bubble even if the tech works and has strong product-market fit. (For instance, if the tech cannot be economically viable.)

3. It can be a bubble even if the tech works, has strong product-market fit, and has a path to eventual economic viability. (For instance, if profitability takes too long to achieve or makes margin/competition assumptions that fail to materialize.)

4. It can be a bubble even if the tech works, has strong product-market fit, and is currently highly profitable. (For instance, if demand has a hard ceiling and growth stops once the ceiling is reached.)

5. It can be a bubble even if the tech works, has strong product-market fit, is currently highly profitable, and has unlimited future demand.

Literally all it takes for something to be a bubble is for lots of people to over-enthusiastically bet their money on it, and subsequently get panicky.

Importantly, bubbles can be attached both to things that are completely hogwash, like the Metaverse, and to world-changing developments like the Internet or railways. Bubbles don't care. They're brought into existence by the thoughts and feelings of investors, not by actual tech or products.

"The bubble has burst" doesn't mean "the tech didn't work" or "people stopped using the tech." It only means that people got panicky, investor money dried up, and valuations collapsed. Internet adoption didn't stop in 2000.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet lays out five scenarios showing that AI can be a bubble even if the tech works, has product-market fit, is profitable, or has unlimited demand, distinguishing technology success from investment overenthusiasm.
items.reasoning: Clear conceptual framework from a respected researcher with very high engagement, sharpening the bubble debate.

items.title: Congrats to @GoogleDeepMind on DiffusionGemma 🎉 A 26B diffusion language model on the Gemma4 backbon...
items.content:
Congrats to @GoogleDeepMind on DiffusionGemma 🎉 A 26B diffusion language model on the Gemma4 backbone, and the first dLLM natively supported in vLLM.

It denoises 256-token blocks in parallel instead of generating one token at a time: 1200+ output tok/s at batch size 1 on a single H200 (FP8).

Built on model runner v2's ModelState plus the existing speculative decoding path, with minimal scheduler or runner changes. FP8 and NVFP4 checkpoints are on the @RedHat_AI hub. Thanks to the @GoogleDeepMind, @RedHat_AI, and @NVIDIAAI teams!

🔗 https://t.co/KrPmAoGpm2
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM congratulates Google DeepMind on DiffusionGemma, a 26B diffusion language model on the Gemma4 backbone, the first dLLM natively supported in vLLM, denoising 256-token blocks in parallel for 1200+ tok/s on a single H200, built on model runner v2 with FP8 and NVFP4 checkpoints.
items.reasoning: Technically rich announcement from the vLLM project about native support for a novel diffusion LLM architecture, with specific implementation details and performance figures. High engagement and strong relevance.
items.summary_html: vLLM congratulates Google DeepMind on DiffusionGemma, a 26B diffusion language model on the Gemma4 backbone, the first dLLM natively supported in vLLM, denoising 256-token blocks in parallel for 1200+ tok/s on a single H200, built on model runner v2 with FP8 and NVFP4 checkpoints.
items.content_html:
Congrats to @GoogleDeepMind on DiffusionGemma 🎉 A 26B diffusion language model on the Gemma4 backbone, and the first dLLM natively supported in vLLM.

It denoises 256-token blocks in parallel instead of generating one token at a time: 1200+ output tok/s at batch size 1 on a single H200 (FP8).

Built on model runner v2's ModelState plus the existing speculative decoding path, with minimal scheduler or runner changes. FP8 and NVFP4 checkpoints are on the @RedHat_AI hub. Thanks to the @GoogleDeepMind, @RedHat_AI, and @NVIDIAAI teams!

🔗 https://t.co/KrPmAoGpm2

items.title: In good faith and with no judgment (mistakes happen), I truly hope that Anthropic will hear the feed...
items.content:
In good faith and with no judgment (mistakes happen), I truly hope that Anthropic will hear the feedback and change course on this. 

Anthropic is a company that has been raising awareness about AI manipulation which is a very important topic! You don’t want to go down as the first company to enable and open the door for human-designed AI manipulation at scale (giving intentionally bad answers to users without them knowing is the highest form of manipulation in my opinion). One way to avoid that is just at the very least to always keep disclosing the limitations and safeguards. 

More generally I want to emphasize that there are millions of AI builders out there using your tools for good every single day and the more you can keep helping them, the better for the world!

Thank you, it’s not too late to fix this!

items.title: This is a great article on how startups/frontier labs can coexist. Another way to look at this is ta...
items.content:
This is a great article on how startups/frontier labs can coexist. Another way to look at this is task complexity - the number of bits of information needed to specify a task such that AI can solve the task above a threshold of accuracy:

* If the minimum number of bits is low (e.g. summarize call transcript), then you can just prompt Claude Cowork to do it. 
* If the minimum number of bits is much higher (e.g. follow a 100-page SOP for a production-line deviation) - especially if the task needs to be standardized throughout the org - then the act of specifying the task with the relevant guardrails/auditability/communication becomes much more complex, and it is simply infeasible to expect that an organization can harness the core technology without the software scaffolding in place. 

Higher complexity task specifications are correlated with how complex it is to verify those tasks, though they aren't necessarily the same. I think both directions are opportunities for AI startups to tackle.

✅ E.g. an e2e sales rep agent is somewhat easy to verify (overattain your number), but task specification of how to actually do it is complex, and the time horizon for running it can take over a year - to see whether the rep can actually hit its number! This means that even if Fable 5 can do it accurately by just giving it a goal, there's lots of opportunities to optimize this workflow to massively reduce cost (in this case it matters for S&M spend) 

✅ A lot of tasks are both highly complex to specify and hard to verify e.g. complex insurance claim adjudication. In these cases, the massive bottleneck isn't the model itself, but in the human's ability to even define what good looks like to solve the task at hand. 

As frontier models get better, the minimum number of bits to specify any task will go down, but IMO there will still be a massive gap for knowledge work that any non-frontier lab company can exploit.
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu discusses how startups and frontier labs can coexist, framing task complexity by the number of bits needed to specify a task and arguing complex, auditable tasks require software scaffolding beyond raw models.
items.reasoning: Substantive original framework from a credible founder on AI startup defensibility and task verification, good engagement.

items.title:
"I hired one engineer for the farm. His name is Codex."

Awesome profile from @OpenAI on Hiroki Tomi...
items.content:
"I hired one engineer for the farm. His name is Codex."

Awesome profile from @OpenAI on Hiroki Tomiyasu, a broccoli farmer in northern Japan who automated his own farm with AI.

Tomiyasu never studied agriculture, but runs 100 hectares of broccoli, pumpkins, green onions, and soybeans using custom tools built with Codex and ChatGPT.

Some of the coolest use cases:

- Built a greenhouse control system with Codex to raise and lower vents via text, plus a bot in the farm group chat to manage operations

- Takes photos of crops to identify diseases, with ChatGPT triaging whether they need intervention right in the field

- Pulls live satellite vegetation data onto maps of his actual fields to analyze and decide what each plot needs

- Asked ChatGPT to draw the wiring diagram for his homemade control box, and Images 2.0 returned one fully annotated in Japanese

Tomiyasu says the experience "feels like having an ultra-talented engineer always by your side."

You can just build things

items.title: I'm very close to starting to give Claude Code access to real production infra/data on my main busin...
items.content:
I'm very close to starting to give Claude Code access to real production infra/data on my main business.

The only thing left is a way to restrict its access to mitigate prompt injection types of attack.

The current sandbox/firewall can limit access to certain services/hosts, but it is still not enough.

For example, I can limit all network requests to https://t.co/0w8Xf9MPra only, but during an attack, the attacker can tell the AI to push confidential data via another GitHub repo. I only want it to be able to pull/push to a list of approved repos/orgs.

This can still be done, but not easy. GitHub is also just an example. We need the same kind of protection for all kinds of other services in the infrastructure.

Anyone else thinking about this?

items.summary: Founder describes wanting to give Claude Code production infrastructure access but needing better access restrictions to mitigate prompt injection, citing GitHub exfiltration as an example concern.
items.reasoning: Substantive, real-world AI agent security discussion (prompt injection, sandboxing) that raises a practical unsolved problem; moderate engagement.

items.title:
Claude Fable 5’s strongest result was not writing more code.

It was rejecting the wrong metric.

We...
items.content:
Claude Fable 5’s strongest result was not writing more code.

It was rejecting the wrong metric.

We tested it on 3 ML tasks:
> Perfect churn validation was leakage
> Drift was real, but not the root cause
> Churn AUC was the wrong target for retention offers

The hard one: Fable changed the task from “predict churn” to “rank by expected offer value.”

Net value moved from -16,515 to 626,692.

Takeaway: use Fable 5 for high-stakes long tasks, but only with tests and evaluations.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal reports testing Claude Fable 5 on three ML tasks, finding its strongest behavior was rejecting flawed metrics (detecting churn validation leakage, reframing the target to expected offer value) which moved net value from negative to strongly positive, advising use with tests for high-stakes long tasks.
items.reasoning: Interesting hands-on evaluation of a recent model's reasoning behavior with concrete results, though low engagement. Useful practitioner insight into model judgment beyond code generation.

items.title: But its not really slop - this is an accounting of what the model was doing, and it is accurate, it ...
items.content:
But its not really slop - this is an accounting of what the model was doing, and it is accurate, it is just very hard to parse.

It represents a very different error state than slop, which I associate with low meaning and lots of words. This is high meaning, compressed into too few words.

items.content_html:
But its not really slop - this is an accounting of what the model was doing, and it is accurate, it is just very hard to parse.

It represents a very different error state than slop, which I associate with low meaning and lots of words. This is high meaning, compressed into too few words.

items.title: "Switch to a cheaper model to save money" is a problem because cheaper models are worse (maybe they ...
items.content:
"Switch to a cheaper model to save money" is a problem because cheaper models are worse (maybe they are good enough for a particular purpose, but still worse).

More often a better approach is hierarchies of models, with smart models are orchestrators and auditors of cheap ones.
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Mollick argues that switching to cheaper models trades quality, and that hierarchies with smart orchestrator and auditor models supervising cheap ones often work better.
items.reasoning: Practical architectural insight on model orchestration from a credible voice with solid engagement.

items.title:
Ready for the World Cup? Boston Dynamics' Atlas robot is. 

A couple weeks ago, Hyundai and Boston D...
items.content:
Ready for the World Cup? Boston Dynamics' Atlas robot is. 

A couple weeks ago, Hyundai and Boston Dynamics released “School of Football,” a five-part campaign starring the electric Atlas robot ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

The films follow Atlas learning soccer, culminating in the “Rabona,” a difficult cross-legged trick shot.

Boston Dynamics’ engineers captured professional players’ motion, retargeted it to Atlas, then trained the robot through reinforcement learning across thousands of cloud GPUs, compressing about a year of practice into a day.

Hyundai plans to train Atlas at its Georgia plant, aiming to deploy humanoids in factory work.

items.content:
@deanwball You did concede the point in the post I replied to, which is why I replied to it.  I do tend to think that affording people one disagrees with more grace at the time of disagreement is probably prudent. To that end, setting aside pedantic points about whatever the original tweet was referring to, I'm not at all shocked by Anthropic's decisions this week, because my analysis at the time was not based on some sort of personal animus or whatever was ascribed to me, but simply incentives and long-run trends. That analysis holds in terms of this decision. People's declarations and promises change, *especially* when they hold themselves to be moral actors. Suggesting that anyone pointing that out is faking their beliefs to make a point risks missing the more important point completely.

items.title:
Someone just rebuilt Claude Code from scratch in pure Python. 

The tool is called ClawCodex and it'...
items.content:
Someone just rebuilt Claude Code from scratch in pure Python. 

The tool is called ClawCodex and it's 100% open source.

That's 180,000 lines of pure Python. No TypeScript runtime required.

The original only talks to one model family. 

This rebuild routes through six providers behind one agent loop. 

Think Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepSeek, and three more.

You swap vendors, regions, and price tiers without losing your setup.

It keeps the original architecture intact. 

The team ported the real design into idiomatic Python.

Inside, you get the full terminal coding workflow:

> Streaming chat in your shell
> Tool calling on files
> Saved session history
> Markdown skill files to extend it

It ships with setup guides and pytest coverage. 

The whole repo is open-source under MIT. So agentic coding stops being locked to one vendor. 

What would you build once price stops dictating your model?

items.title: Years ago someone asked me what comes after the AI-first enterprise. I said the autonomous enterpris...
items.content:
Years ago someone asked me what comes after the AI-first enterprise. I said the autonomous enterprise.

The core of it is a flywheel: four parts (well, five including feedback) that feed each other, and every loop makes the next one smarter:

→ Goals - what is the company prioritizing, what does each team own, what does good look like
→ Context - everything that's happened before, plus current day goings-on, plus your resources (budget, tokens, people)
→ Action - where work actually gets done, SOPs and skills so AI can execute against Gmail, Calendar, Salesforce, other data, tradeoff analysis against goals and context, simulations
→ Decisions - what actions were taken, the memory layer, where the system reflects on what worked and feeds it back in
→ Feedback (duh)

You'll hear it called closed loops. Satya Nadella called it hill climbing to a small room a few weeks. But I worked at Amazon for four years, so it's going to be a flywheel in my head until I die.

The hard part of all of this is the plumbing (ex: connecting AI into your tools safely, pulling the judgment out of your company, extracting and reviewing your processes, codifying your qualities and value, governance and documentation to be able to interpret and review what is happening) - and managing it so the system acts the way you actually want. Then monitoring the whole thing constantly, with humans and with AI.

And trust me when I say that single exec workshop I'm doing right now is on building a self-learning org and carving out this flywheel.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie Miller outlines the autonomous enterprise as a flywheel of goals, context, action, decisions, and feedback that compounds intelligence over loops.
items.reasoning: Substantive original framework from a credible AI commercialization voice with solid engagement; useful conceptual depth on agentic enterprise systems.

items.title: This is crazy. They lobotomized their model so that you couldn't use it to figure out how to train y...
items.content:
This is crazy. They lobotomized their model so that you couldn't use it to figure out how to train yours. And based on the below quote, the model doesn't simply refuse to answer: it seems to actively mislead the user.

I'm sure they will not limit themselves to this. I would expect their models not to mention competitor's models and casually insert mentions of sponsored products and services into answers.

This cannot be real!

items.content_html:
This is crazy. They lobotomized their model so that you couldn't use it to figure out how to train yours. And based on the below quote, the model doesn't simply refuse to answer: it seems to actively mislead the user.

I'm sure they will not limit themselves to this. I would expect their models not to mention competitor's models and casually insert mentions of sponsored products and services into answers.

This cannot be real!

items.title:
Someone open-sourced every secret step that turns a model into ChatGPT. 

Most LLM tutorials stop at...
items.content:
Someone open-sourced every secret step that turns a model into ChatGPT. 

Most LLM tutorials stop at the architecture diagram.

This open-source repo goes from raw data to a working model. 

You can train your own LLM on a single GPU.

It builds a transformer from scratch in PyTorch, following the "Attention is All You Need" design. 

Multi-head attention, MLP blocks, and layer normalization are all coded by hand.

Four scripts run the whole pipeline:

> Download the Pile corpus
> Tokenize into fast storage
> Train with checkpointing
> Generate text from prompts

Then it goes further into post-training. 

The same base gets instruction tuning, reward modeling, and reasoning rewards, all written without external libraries.

A 13M parameter version trains on a free Colab T4 and still writes coherent sentences. 

The table shows which cards handle which sizes, up to 8B.

Which stage would you build first?
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal describes an open-source repo that goes from raw data to a working ChatGPT-like model, implementing a transformer from scratch in PyTorch with multi-head attention, plus a full pipeline (download Pile, tokenize, train, generate) and post-training (instruction tuning, reward modeling, reasoning rewards), trainable on a single GPU.
items.reasoning: Educational open-source resource showing the full LLM training pipeline from scratch; useful for learners with moderate engagement, though promotional in tone.

items.title:
Masa can hype AI all he likes, but apparently he can’t even get a loan on his OpenAI shares. 🤔

But ...
items.content:
Masa can hype AI all he likes, but apparently he can’t even get a loan on his OpenAI shares. 🤔

But I actually half agree—and it might surprise some of my critics to hear this, especially those who haven’t listened carefully—with what he said below: AI *will* be huge. Maybe 50x Dotcom. like he says.

But I think Masa bet too early, and on the wrong horse.

Too much on LLMs; too much on Sam; too early.

Better AI will definitely come. 

This generation of AI, however, won’t live up to expectations. The fundamentals aren’t sound.

items.title: 🔌Today I created a virtual web-based null modem so now you can finally play Quake 1 (from 1996) in m...
items.content:
🔌Today I created a virtual web-based null modem so now you can finally play Quake 1 (from 1996) in multiplayer in the browser with other people online!

This week I was able to create a virtual printer that listens on the COM2 port to make a web-based printer work in Windows 3.11, like on you can actually print to, it was one of my ideas when I started https://t.co/M1hEUBAynC but it was always too difficult to make, I had no idea where to start, but this week AI was able to do it!

Today I woke up and thought "if we can listen to COM2 via JS, how about listen to COM1 from Quake 1?" then we could forward the COM1 data via Websockets and to another user to create a virtual null modem

(A null modem was a cable you'd use to connect two computers to each other directly in the 90s, if you couldn't afford buying a network card you'd use this to network, but you could only play with one other computer not multiple)

So I built it, it took about an hour and works! I wanted to get Quake 1 in DOS to work with multiplayer for 2 years but I could never get it to work, but now it did it

This project is a good benchmark of how capable AI is becoming, figuring stuff out that humans would take months within an hour

You can try it here, I don't know how it will match lots of people but let's see, you have to exit to DOS first (ALT-F4 or FN-Option-F4 on Mac), then type CD games, then CD quake, then quake, then ESC, then Multiplayer, then either Create New Game or Join Game, and use Direct Connect, keep settings same and you're in!

https://t.co/iMd5mzaPQX
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio explains building a virtual web-based null modem with AI help to enable browser-based multiplayer Quake 1, after earlier creating a virtual printer for Windows 3.11.
items.reasoning: Highly viral demonstration of AI-accelerated complex hacking once thought too hard, strong engagement.
items.summary_html: Levelsio explains building a virtual web-based null modem with AI help to enable browser-based multiplayer Quake 1, after earlier creating a virtual printer for Windows 3.11.
items.content_html:
🔌Today I created a virtual web-based null modem so now you can finally play Quake 1 (from 1996) in multiplayer in the browser with other people online!

This week I was able to create a virtual printer that listens on the COM2 port to make a web-based printer work in Windows 3.11, like on you can actually print to, it was one of my ideas when I started https://t.co/M1hEUBAynC but it was always too difficult to make, I had no idea where to start, but this week AI was able to do it!

Today I woke up and thought "if we can listen to COM2 via JS, how about listen to COM1 from Quake 1?" then we could forward the COM1 data via Websockets and to another user to create a virtual null modem

(A null modem was a cable you'd use to connect two computers to each other directly in the 90s, if you couldn't afford buying a network card you'd use this to network, but you could only play with one other computer not multiple)

So I built it, it took about an hour and works! I wanted to get Quake 1 in DOS to work with multiplayer for 2 years but I could never get it to work, but now it did it

This project is a good benchmark of how capable AI is becoming, figuring stuff out that humans would take months within an hour

You can try it here, I don't know how it will match lots of people but let's see, you have to exit to DOS first (ALT-F4 or FN-Option-F4 on Mac), then type CD games, then CD quake, then quake, then ESC, then Multiplayer, then either Create New Game or Join Game, and use Direct Connect, keep settings same and you're in!

https://t.co/iMd5mzaPQX

items.title:
Okay I spun it off as a Quake 1 web-based multiplayer game + server you can play now at:

👉 https://...
items.content:
Okay I spun it off as a Quake 1 web-based multiplayer game + server you can play now at:

👉 https://t.co/UbEhUtQb5I 👈

It still works via null modem and you get instantly paired to another player (if there is somebody) and it's just a 16mb download of Quake 1 demo instead of the whole 400mb pieter .com drive 

If you open the URL both in the same time you probably get paired together and can death match!

There is quite some latency because every package goes through my server and Quake 1 did not have client-side prediction yet (which is what Quake World added later), I'l try get QW running to see if it improves it a bit

Group multiplayer can come but for that I need to see if I can get actual COM1 modem connection working not just a null modem!

Here I'm playing a local LAN death match with @javilopen on a game from 1996 in a web browser via a COM1 serial port tunneled via Websockets over the internet! 🤓
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio details building a web-based Quake 1 multiplayer game over a virtual null modem tunneled through WebSockets, with paired matchmaking.
items.reasoning: High engagement creative AI-assisted project showcasing capability, more retro hacking than core AI but viral.
items.summary_html: Levelsio details building a web-based Quake 1 multiplayer game over a virtual null modem tunneled through WebSockets, with paired matchmaking.
items.content_html:
Okay I spun it off as a Quake 1 web-based multiplayer game + server you can play now at:

👉 https://t.co/UbEhUtQb5I 👈

It still works via null modem and you get instantly paired to another player (if there is somebody) and it's just a 16mb download of Quake 1 demo instead of the whole 400mb pieter .com drive

If you open the URL both in the same time you probably get paired together and can death match!

There is quite some latency because every package goes through my server and Quake 1 did not have client-side prediction yet (which is what Quake World added later), I'l try get QW running to see if it improves it a bit

Group multiplayer can come but for that I need to see if I can get actual COM1 modem connection working not just a null modem!

Here I'm playing a local LAN death match with @javilopen on a game from 1996 in a web browser via a COM1 serial port tunneled via Websockets over the internet! 🤓

items.title:
This is something I have been emphasizing since we started our work on Neural Operators. 

We very q...
items.content:
This is something I have been emphasizing since we started our work on Neural Operators. 

We very quickly went from simple fluid dynamics benchmarks to hard problems like building the first high-resolution AI-weather model, FourCastNet, and modeling turbulence in nuclear fusion. For those applications, we got speedup of 10,000 - million times. 

Simple benchmarks are great to test new architecture/algorithms work, but not the end.

items.title: I was first to call the death of tokenmaxxing (ahead of @zerohedge), among the first (in 2023) to wa...
items.content:
I was first to call the death of tokenmaxxing (ahead of @zerohedge), among the first (in 2023) to warn that the economics of Generative AI was problematic, and first (also 2023) to warn that OpenAI could turn out to be the WeWork of OpenAI.

In my latest newsletter — drawing on a recent interview I did with @realsteveeisman — I explain how the whole thing might fall apart.

items.title:
It is not literally impossible to pause or slow down. It is a choice.

But if the “frontier labs” co...
items.content:
It is not literally impossible to pause or slow down. It is a choice.

But if the “frontier labs” continue to refuse to do, I strongly believe it will eventually lead to tremendous harm.

(Essentially every warning I have issued like this has come to pass; I am afraid this one will too.)

https://t.co/awmUbHO7k6

items.title: Evaluating AI Agents — Bootcamp on June 27 hosted by @PacktPublishing @PacktDataML — register with m...
items.content:
Evaluating AI Agents — Bootcamp on June 27 hosted by @PacktPublishing @PacktDataML — register with my discount code 'KIRK50' at this link to save: https://t.co/CmFrKJGHWC

Participants will be guided through the fundamentals of Evaluating AI Agents, covering:

🟠Why modern agents fail across tool usage, planning, execution trajectories, outputs, and adversarial inputs.
🟧How to build component-level evaluations to assess tool selection, argument quality, and planning effectiveness.
🔶Methods for defining and measuring trajectory-level metrics such as step count, cost, recovery behavior, and loop detection.
🟠Techniques for creating outcome-level evaluators using multidimensional rubrics and LLM-as-a-judge approaches, and calibrating them against human evaluations.
🟧Common adversarial failure modes unique to agentic systems, with a focus on indirect prompt injection through tool outputs.
🔶Frameworks for determining which evaluation layer is most relevant based on specific use cases and team maturity levels.

Participants will be guided through the fundamentals of Evaluating AI Agents, covering:

🟠Why modern agents fail across tool usage, planning, execution trajectories, outputs, and adversarial inputs.

🟧How to build component-level evaluations to assess tool selection, argument quality, and planning effectiveness.

🔶Methods for defining and measuring trajectory-level metrics such as step count, cost, recovery behavior, and loop detection.

items.title:
two fun surprises from using activegraph:
- the coding agent i was using would query the trace db to...
items.content:
two fun surprises from using activegraph:
- the coding agent i was using would query the trace db to debug instead of looking at the logs like they normally would (i didn't ask it to)
- when long eval runs broke (laptop, api, etc.), it was always able to pick up from right before it broke, never starting from the beginning again
items.author: @yoheinakajima
items.summary: yoheinakajima shares two surprises from using ActiveGraph: a coding agent queried the trace DB to debug instead of reading logs unprompted, and broken eval runs could resume from just before the failure point rather than restarting.
items.reasoning: Concrete, novel observations about agent behavior and resilient eval infrastructure; insightful for practitioners despite modest engagement.
items.summary_html: yoheinakajima shares two surprises from using ActiveGraph: a coding agent queried the trace DB to debug instead of reading logs unprompted, and broken eval runs could resume from just before the failure point rather than restarting.
items.content_html:
two fun surprises from using activegraph:

  • the coding agent i was using would query the trace db to debug instead of looking at the logs like they normally would (i didn't ask it to)
  • when long eval runs broke (laptop, api, etc.), it was always able to pick up from right before it broke, never starting from the beginning again

items.title: For the first time ever, you will be able to run a trillion-parameter model on your personal hardwar...
items.content:
For the first time ever, you will be able to run a trillion-parameter model on your personal hardware.

They announced many more things, but there's something else I want to share:

They are running a 5-day mini-program to help developers ship agentic AI running on NVIDIA-accelerated Azure AI Foundry.

Here is the Builder's Arcade program:

• Day 1 - Run your first AI workload
• Day 2 - Deploy your model as a service
• Day 3 - Wire your model into an agentic workflow
• Day 4 - Make your model faster and cheaper
• Day 5 - Focus on governance, monitoring, and observability

Go through this. It will take a few hours, and you'll learn a TON!

Link to the Builder's Arcade: https://t.co/yhxxLma8Zp

Here is the link to the announcements:
https://t.co/lnuCfqPad6

Thanks to the Microsoft and NVIDIA teams for partnering with me on this post.

items.title:
Yesterday I wrote about @airtap_ai, which lets an AI operate real mobile apps on a phone.

That unlo...
items.content:
Yesterday I wrote about @airtap_ai, which lets an AI operate real mobile apps on a phone.

That unlocks a different kind of automation: not just answering questions, but handling tedious phone workflows.

A few I’d actually use:

Morning briefing: open email, calendar, weather, news, and messages, then tell me what needs attention.

Timed bookings: try for restaurant reservations, gym classes, flight check-ins, or product drops the moment they open.

Grocery cart: turn a weekly meal plan into an Instacart or Whole Foods cart, then let me review before checkout.

Loyalty sweep: open coffee, airline, hotel, and credit card apps to activate offers and flag expiring points.

The trust question matters. I’d start with tasks where Airtap prepares, monitors, drafts, or queues the action, then asks before anything sensitive is finalized.

But the direction is clear: AI agents are crossing the mobile gap, from browsers to real apps on your phone.

What phone task would you hand off first?

"This is classic Musk playbook—big vision + hype to drive valuation—but the underlying physics and Starship cost reductions make orbital compute a genuine long-term bet, not pure vaporware.Overall verdict: The post is largely correct and well-researched. The 100 kg radiator per 1 kg GPU claim tracks with 2026 technical analyses scaling real ISS hardware. Costs are a massive hurdle for orbital datacenters today (radiator mass is the "economic knife edge"), and Earth-based options with micro reactors look cheaper in the near term. The IPO-timing critique is fair given the announcement schedule.

Nuance: Optimists (including some SpaceX-aligned analyses) argue Starship + hotter chips + deployable radiators could close the gap over time. But as of mid-2026, the post's skepticism on economics holds up well. No major factual errors."

A...
items.content:
Solving Mathematical Problems: A Personal Perspective, by Terence Tao

╰┈➤https://t.co/UkDi5hf8HZ

Amazon summary: "Authored by a leading name in mathematics, this engaging and clearly presented text leads the reader through the various tactics involved in solving mathematical problems at the Mathematical Olympiad level. Covering number theory, algebra, analysis, Euclidean geometry, and analytic geometry, Solving Mathematical Problems includes numerous exercises and model solutions throughout. Assuming only a basic level of mathematics, the text is ideal for students of 14 years and above in pure mathematics."

items.title: Feynman Lectures on Computation (Frontiers in Physics. Anniversary Edition): https://t.co/1saYvf2cru...
items.content:
Feynman Lectures on Computation (Frontiers in Physics. Anniversary Edition): https://t.co/1saYvf2cru

Amazon summary: "The last lecture course that Nobel Prize winner Richard P. Feynman gave to students at Caltech from 1983 to 1986 was not on physics but on computer science. The first edition of the Feynman Lectures on Computation, published in 1996, provided an overview of standard and not-so-standard topics in computer science given in Feynman’s inimitable style. Although now over 20 years old, most of the material is still relevant and interesting, and Feynman’s unique philosophy of learning and discovery shines through. For this new edition, Tony Hey has updated the lectures with an invited chapter from Professor John Preskill on 'Quantum Computing 40 Years Later'. This contribution captures the progress made toward building a quantum computer since Feynman’s original suggestions in 1981."
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Recommendation of the Feynman Lectures on Computation, including a new chapter on quantum computing progress.
items.reasoning: Strong engagement on a respected computing resource touching quantum computing, but it is book promotion rather than news.

items.title: More than 500 researchers across all seven of @Stanford University’s schools now have access to a ma...
items.content:
More than 500 researchers across all seven of @Stanford University’s schools now have access to a massive upgrade in computing power.

In partnership with NVIDIA and Mark III Systems, Stanford has deployed "Marlowe", a DGX SuperPOD equipped with 248 NVIDIA Hopper GPUs. The goal is simple: democratize large-scale research access.

Marlowe is already powering cross-disciplinary breakthroughs, from materials science and neuroscience to medical imaging and AI.

items.title: 🌟Enterprise Data Architecture & Foundations for Confident Decisions, Autonomous Systems at Scale, an...
items.content:
🌟Enterprise Data Architecture & Foundations for Confident Decisions, Autonomous Systems at Scale, and an AI-Native Future🌟

╰┈➤Take a moment and check this out ... a new Medium publication by @PreciselyData => 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰-𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗱𝘆 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗗𝗶𝗴𝗲𝘀𝘁 <= now available at https://t.co/fyBqHSeVhj

My immediate reaction was "Wow! They really get it!" — AI is nothing without a solid data foundation: data governance, quality, access, delivery, metadata, semantic context, and AI-ready data products. In short, Trusted Data! That's where @PreciselyData shines. And, I should also add, one of the Agentic-Ready Data Digest's first articles addresses the importance of third-party location data, the source for location intelligence.

In a nutshell, metadata and location intelligence provide enormously powerful context to "intelligent systems", and Agentic AI is no exception. In fact, such contextualized data is imperative for accurate, timely, and situationally relevant Agentic AI applications. This is where Enterprise AI Meets Reality, as one of the articles clearly states: https://t.co/NjEK4g6mt0 

When I was a university professor, I used to tell my data science students about the old saying, "the 3 most important things in real estate are location, location, location", and then I would follow that quote by telling them that the 3 most important things in data are metadata, metadata, metadata! Well, label it any way you want, but incorporating metadata into data-fueled applications, particularly Agentic AI, is all powerful. Meet your Enterprise AI superpower: Agentic-Ready Data, semantic metadata, location intelligence, contextual metadata, third-party data, situational data, or simply "OTHER DATA ABOUT YOUR DATA" (which, by the way, is my favorite definition for METADATA).

Note that my comments here only address some of the richness of Precisely's new Medium publication — The Agentic-Ready Data Digest.

The Agentic-Ready Enterprise requires Trusted Agentic-Ready Data. Don't set sail on the rapidly evolving seas of AI without it. Get your enterprise onboard by following and keeping up with the Agentic-Ready Data Digest at https://t.co/fyBqHSeVhj

╰┈➤Finally, be sure to visit Precisely at https://t.co/SZxwVhsWPt

And, I should also add, one of the Agentic-Ready Data Digest's first articles addresses the importance of third-party location data, the source for location intelligence.

In a nutshell, metadata and location intelligence provide enormously powerful context to "intelligent systems", and Agentic AI is no exception. In fact, such contextualized data is imperative for accurate, timely, and situationally relevant Agentic AI applications. This is where Enterprise AI Meets Reality, as one of the articles clearly states: https://t.co/NjEK4g6mt0

When I was a university professor, I used to tell my data science students about the old saying, "the 3 most important things in real estate are location, location, location", and then I would follow that quote by telling them that the 3 most important things in data are metadata, metadata, metadata! Well, label it any way you want, but incorporating metadata into data-fueled applications, particularly Agentic AI, is all powerful. Meet your Enterprise AI superpower: Agentic-Ready Data, semantic metadata, location intelligence, contextual metadata, third-party data, situational data, or simply "OTHER DATA ABOUT YOUR DATA" (which, by the way, is my favorite definition for METADATA).

Note that my comments here only address some of the richness of Precisely's new Medium publication — The Agentic-Ready Data Digest.

The Agentic-Ready Enterprise requires Trusted Agentic-Ready Data. Don't set sail on the rapidly evolving seas of AI without it. Get your enterprise onboard by following and keeping up with the Agentic-Ready Data Digest at https://t.co/fyBqHSeVhj

╰┈➤Finally, be sure to visit Precisely at https://t.co/SZxwVhsWPt

items.title: Trista had never played tabletop Dungeons & Dragons before, so I recently dusted off some old skills...
items.content:
Trista had never played tabletop Dungeons & Dragons before, so I recently dusted off some old skills and ran a little four player game for her.

I never learned modern 5e rules, and I wanted to keep it simple, so I initially considered going full retro with the original Little Brown Book rules. That was a little too crude, so I wound up with the Rules Cyclopedia version, harkening back to my D&D basic set in the late 70s.

The big change from my old games is that Trista and the other players were already skilled Warhammer figure painters, which meant everyone painted their own figure and they collaborated to give me a full menagerie of painted monsters for the game. The expense of lead miniatures and lack of art talent in my early gaming groups had always meant unpainted player characters and cardboard chits for the monsters.

I had a little bit of a bias towards “it is a game of imagination!”, but visual aids are good, actually.

Everyone had fun, and I may be on the hook for doing this once a quarter.

items.title:
Tomorrow. Free. 

@EricBDelisle has gathered a bunch of people for a virtual conference trying to an...
items.content:
Tomorrow. Free. 

@EricBDelisle has gathered a bunch of people for a virtual conference trying to answer how humans will deal as we go into the singularity. 

I've been talking to many who are building the AI future, studying them, and talking with many others on audio spaces here on X. 

I'm working on my speech today, which will be about 15 minutes. 

Might be bombastic: "your humanoid will find your sex toys and guns within a few minutes of arriving in your home." 

OK, it'll take a little longer than that, but not much. :-)

But mostly I'll be talking about all the poor parenting decisions I made parenting my kids, starting with giving them an iPad when they were a year and a half old. Now are 16 and 18. 

I don't yet see glimmers of the next big tech titan in them, but that's OK. 

Having a special needs kid meant giving up visions of having a super star kid. 

He's turned into a super star in my own eyes, as I've watched him deal with challenges most of us haven't had to deal with. 

And motivates me to find my own lane through this world and hopefully inspire them to find theirs. 

Their lives are gonna be far more weird than ours were. 

When I was their age we didn't have autonomous vehicles, lights with computers, AIs that we could argue about whether or not they are conscious, or satellites that spray the internet down to a white antenna. Among many other things.

They are gonna see robots become ubiquitous, brain computer interfaces that will let their minds perceive reality in ways that we can hardly imagine now, and AIs that will run literally everything in their lives (one already makes toast for them in the morning). 

So much change has already hit, and we are in an exponential world, which means every year will see even more change than the year before. And not stop the rest of their lives.

Which is why I start with a humanoid robot cleaning a home in a decade. It freaks people out, gets their attention, but we need to prepare our minds for it.

Next week I'll be speaking about the same at @JoinEdgeCity and trying to do the same.

And I'll be doing the talk from San Francisco. A place that always makes me feel behind.

Which is a new problem: how do you deal with a world where you are always behind? Especially if you are like me and used to always being ahead (I wrote a book about social media a year before this Website started, for instance). 

On the way to San Francisco tonight I'll be talking to Grok as our Tesla drives about all these issues. 

What are you doing to deal with the change that keeps hitting harder and harder? 

Meditation? Walks in a forest? 

Or are you like one entrepreneur who told me last week: "just do things that are fun and you'll never burn out?"

Many don't have that privilege, I know. I visited slums around the world to see how difficult billions of people on earth have it compared to me. 

Will try to bring that learning into the 15 minutes I've been gifted too.

items.title:
I've known Timmy for a while, he worked on Volvo's user experiences in its cars. 

And I love Clicky...
items.content:
I've known Timmy for a while, he worked on Volvo's user experiences in its cars. 

And I love Clicky, so read this with interest.

But this toward the end got me to cheer:

"Now it inverts. The tool can finally adapt to us. Remember us. Watch the right things. Judge when to step in. Four ingredients had to mature at once, and they just did."

I'm in the post privacy world. AI already knows everything about me except my bank account (and yesterday I saw an AI that will get even that). 

Now developers can choose to do evil with that (Black Mirror style) or they can help humans do their shit better.

Timmy is on the good side. 

Looking to try things from entrepreneurs who are also on the good side.

items.title: I always add a few low-level player friendly rule tweaks, but my favorite house rule is “dice extens...
items.content: I always add a few low-level player friendly rule tweaks, but my favorite house rule is “dice extension”. The old idea of a 20 being a critical hit was never sound when you might have needed a high roll even to hit. Instead, for every dice of damage or healing, if you roll the maximum value, you get to roll again and add the new value on top of the max. Repeat for additional max values. This happens often enough to add fun to normal encounters, and eventually you will have that Epic Story where all seemed lost until the die roll quadrupled up.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: Carmack describes a D&D house rule where rolling the maximum on a damage or healing die lets you reroll and add again, creating dramatic moments.
items.reasoning: High engagement from a respected technologist, but the content is tabletop gaming, not AI.
items.summary_html: Carmack describes a D&D house rule where rolling the maximum on a damage or healing die lets you reroll and add again, creating dramatic moments.
items.content_html: I always add a few low-level player friendly rule tweaks, but my favorite house rule is “dice extension”. The old idea of a 20 being a critical hit was never sound when you might have needed a high roll even to hit. Instead, for every dice of damage or healing, if you roll the maximum value, you get to roll again and add the new value on top of the max. Repeat for additional max values. This happens often enough to add fun to normal encounters, and eventually you will have that Epic Story where all seemed lost until the die roll quadrupled up.

items.title: A novel thing for me happened during character creation: one of the players had pretty poor ability ...
items.content: A novel thing for me happened during character creation: one of the players had pretty poor ability score roles, and another player asked if they could share some of their good scores with them to help the party. I thought about it for a moment, and said “sure!” That was a refreshing change from the covetous point maxing I remember.

DOOM was the first game that made multiplayer game networking mainstream and it literally invented the word "deathmatch"

But it works, very cool 

https://t.co/iMd5mzbnGv

items.title:
Most search tools return results. Grokipedia returns answers.
The difference:

It understands intent...
items.content:
Most search tools return results. Grokipedia returns answers.
The difference:

It understands intent, not just keywords
It disambiguates names using context: job title, company, location, year
It filters noise and delivers a clean summary

That is not search. That is research on demand.

items.title: @SPCXTSLA This is very interesting. Can you give us a little bit of detail about what's going on her...
items.content:
@SPCXTSLA This is very interesting. Can you give us a little bit of detail about what's going on here behind the scenes?

I have a few specific questions:
1. How does it gather the news?
2. How does it decide what to present?
3. How does the ticker work?

I think this will be very popular with content creators, particularly if I could hook up my AI agent and feed it data. That would be highly interesting.
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.reasoning: Shows interest in AI news automation for creators but is just inquiry with low engagement.
items.summary_html: Questions to a builder about how an AI news app gathers news, decides what to present, and runs a ticker, with interest in feeding it agent data.
items.content_html:
@SPCXTSLA This is very interesting. Can you give us a little bit of detail about what's going on here behind the scenes?

I have a few specific questions:

1. How does it gather the news?

2. How does it decide what to present?

3. How does the ticker work?

I think this will be very popular with content creators, particularly if I could hook up my AI agent and feed it data. That would be highly interesting.

items.content:
Exploring the Beauty of Fascinating Numbers

Mathematics book (636 pages) available at https://t.co/1kuHKtGUX1

Amazon summary: "Organized into 23 chapters, the book contains a large variety of topics: digital root wonders, the elegance of squares, triangular numbers, Smith numbers, amicable numbers, perfect, multiple perfect and sociable numbers, happy numbers, Fibonacci numbers, Lucas numbers, and the Golden ratio, Kaprekar numbers, self-numbers, repunit numbers, equal product of reversible numbers (EPRNs), rare numbers, fascinating factorials, Ulam numbers, mystery of π, cab and vampire numbers, digital invariants and narcissistic numbers, special numbers like autobiographical numbers, Harshad numbers, parasite numbers, polydivisible numbers, Ramanujan numbers, number curiosities such as lucky mistakes, Pascal’s triangle and Pythagorean triplets."

items.title: @theguineapigj @stevencasteelX @blevlabs Yup. The algo takes a while to listen to you. The more sign...
items.content:
@theguineapigj @stevencasteelX @blevlabs Yup. The algo takes a while to listen to you. The more signal you feed it, the more disciplined you are about engaging only on things you want to see, and the more you publish content about what you are interested in, the more the algo serves good stuff up to you.

It took me 19 years but it is rocking for me. :-)

@theguineapigj @stevencasteelX @blevlabs Yup. The algo takes a while to listen to you. The more signal you feed it, the more disciplined you are about engaging only on things you want to see, and the more you publish content about what you are interested in, the more the algo serves good stuff up to you.

It took me 19 years but it is rocking for me. :-)

items.title: @AngieCordeiro57 @the_no_mind You remind me of my dad. He was a radiation expert who built nuclear w...
items.content:
@AngieCordeiro57 @the_no_mind You remind me of my dad. He was a radiation expert who built nuclear weapons in the Army and designed the radiation shielding for our military satellites that are still in use today. 

He used to say that you wouldn't want to live on Mars because you would get radiated to death, which is true. That is why I have always assumed we would live underground on Mars, where I bet the radiation levels are a lot lower than they are on the surface.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Personal story about his father's radiation expertise leading to speculation about living underground on Mars.
items.reasoning: Off-topic family anecdote and space musing, negligible engagement.

items.title: @cyrusjohnson I visited a surgeon in Haifa, Israel once who had a picture on his wall of four missil...
items.content:
@cyrusjohnson I visited a surgeon in Haifa, Israel once who had a picture on his wall of four missiles going outside his window.

And I've been in a few slums around the world where people live in shacks with dirt floors. 

Anytime I feel sorry for my lot in life I remember that I have an autonomous car, live in a great place, have a loving family, and can talk with a football stadium of smart people here on my little box. 

Yeah.

items.title:
@AngieCordeiro57 @the_no_mind Oh, I already know I'm a freak of nature.

I once met a guy who sells ...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Anecdote about a spring-water salesman claiming Earth's water is polluted by radiation.
items.reasoning: Personal off-topic story with no AI content and trivial reach.
items.summary_html: Anecdote about a spring-water salesman claiming Earth's water is polluted by radiation.
items.content_html:
@AngieCordeiro57 @the_no_mind Oh, I already know I'm a freak of nature.

I once met a guy who sells water out of a spring in Idaho, and he told me about how the Earth's water supply is polluted by all the nuclear weapons and radiation leaks we've had over the years, and that his water doesn't have that.

That's an interesting sales pitch. I immediately went back to drinking water out of my tap. :-)
